title: Ford–Fulkerson algorithm
content:
The Ford–Fulkerson method or Ford–Fulkerson algorithm (FFA) is a greedy algorithm that computes the maximum flow in a flow network. It is sometimes called a "method" instead of an "algorithm" as the approach to finding augmenting paths in a residual graph is not fully specified or it is specified in several implementations with different running times. It was published in 1956 by L. R. Ford Jr. and D. R. Fulkerson. The name "Ford–Fulkerson" is often also used for the Edmonds–Karp algorithm, which is a fully defined implementation of the Ford–Fulkerson method.

The idea behind the algorithm is as follows: as long as there is a path from the source (start node) to the sink (end node), with available capacity on all edges in the path, we send flow along one of the paths. Then we find another path, and so on. A path with available capacity is called an augmenting path. Algorithm. Let formula_1 be a graph, and for each edge from to, let formula_2 be the capacity and formula_3 be the flow. We want to find the maximum flow from the source to the sink.

After every step in the algorithm the following is maintained: This means that the flow through the network is a "legal flow" after each round in the algorithm. We define the residual network formula_4 to be the network with capacity formula_5 and no flow. Notice that it can happen that a flow from to is allowed in the residual network, though disallowed in the original network: if formula_6 and formula_7 then formula_8. The path in step 2 can be found with, for example, a breadth-first search (BFS) or a depth-first search in formula_4. If you use the former, the algorithm is called Edmonds–Karp.

When no more paths in step 2 can be found, will not be able to reach in the residual network. If is the set of nodes reachable by in the residual network, then the total capacity in the original network of edges from to the remainder of is on the one hand equal to the total flow we found from to, and on the other hand serves as an upper bound for all such flows. This proves that the flow we found is maximal. See also Max-flow Min-cut theorem. If the graph formula_1 has multiple sources and sinks, we act as follows: Suppose that formula_21 and formula_22.

Add a new source formula_23 with an edge formula_24 from formula_25 to every node formula_26, with capacity formula_27. And add a new sink formula_28 with an edge formula_29 from every node formula_30 to formula_31, with capacity formula_32. Then apply the Ford–Fulkerson algorithm. Also, if a node has capacity constraint formula_33, we replace this node with two nodes formula_34, and an edge formula_35, with capacity formula_36. Then apply the Ford–Fulkerson algorithm. Complexity. By adding the flow augmenting path to the flow already established in the graph, the maximum flow will be reached when no more flow augmenting paths can be found in the graph.

However, there is no certainty that this situation will ever be reached, so the best that can be guaranteed is that the answer will be correct if the algorithm terminates. In the case that the algorithm runs forever, the flow might not even converge towards the maximum flow. However, this situation only occurs with irrational flow values. When the capacities are integers, the runtime of Ford–Fulkerson is bounded by formula_37 (see big O notation), where formula_38 is the number of edges in the graph and formula_39 is the maximum flow in the graph.

This is because each augmenting path can be found in formula_40 time and increases the flow by an integer amount of at least formula_41, with the upper bound formula_39. A variation of the Ford–Fulkerson algorithm with guaranteed termination and a runtime independent of the maximum flow value is the Edmonds–Karp algorithm, which runs in formula_43 time. Integral example. The following example shows the first steps of Ford–Fulkerson in a flow network with 4 nodes, source formula_44 and sink formula_45. This example shows the worst-case behaviour of the algorithm. In each step, only a flow of formula_41 is sent across the network. If breadth-first-search were used instead, only two steps would be needed.

Notice how flow is "pushed back" from formula_47 to formula_48 when finding the path formula_49. Non-terminating example. Consider the flow network shown on the right, with source formula_50, sink formula_51, capacities of edges formula_52, formula_53 and formula_54 respectively formula_41, formula_56 and formula_41 and the capacity of all other edges some integer formula_58. The constant formula_59 was chosen so, that formula_60. We use augmenting paths according to the following table, where formula_61, formula_62 and formula_63. Note that after step 1 as well as after step 5, the residual capacities of edges formula_52, formula_53 and formula_54 are in the form formula_67, formula_68 and formula_69, respectively, for some formula_70.

This means that we can use augmenting paths formula_71, formula_72, formula_71 and formula_74 infinitely many times and residual capacities of these edges will always be in the same form. Total flow in the network after step 5 is formula_75. If we continue to use augmenting paths as above, the total flow converges to formula_76. However, note that there is a flow of value formula_77, by sending formula_78 units of flow along formula_79, 1 unit of flow along formula_80, and formula_78 units of flow along formula_82. Therefore, the algorithm never terminates and the flow does not even converge to the maximum flow.

Another non-terminating example based on the Euclidean algorithm is given by, where they also show that the worst case running-time of the Ford-Fulkerson algorithm on a network formula_1 in ordinal numbers is formula_84. Python implementation of Edmonds–Karp algorithm. import collections class Graph: """This class represents a directed graph using adjacency matrix representation.""" def __init__(self, graph): self.graph = graph # residual graph self.row = len(graph) def bfs(self, s, t, parent): """Returns true if there is a path from source's' to sink't' in residual graph. Also fills parent[] to store the path."""
